schools.summary: Jehu is the son of Hanani the seer who rebuked Asa; still young, he later rebukes Jehoshaphat and writes his annals.
Henry: God warns before he strikes, that Baasha might repent, or that the coming ruin might appear plainly as his justice.
Gill: Jehu is a young son of a prophet then living, later a reprover of kings and a writer of history.
Clarke: little is known of Jehu; his father Hanani was imprisoned for his faithful ministry to Asa.
The oracle mirrors Ahijah's against Jeroboam; Baasha had a probation he neglected, persevering in the sins he had once punished.
God raised Baasha 'out of the dust' by providence; the crown was a gift, yet the treachery and idolatry were his own.
Clarke: God 'made' him prince only by permission; Scripture ascribes to God what his providence suffers to be done.
Gill: the posterity of Baasha is to be wholly cut off by death, none remaining in any branch.
Henry: those who resemble others in sin resemble them in punishment; Baasha's house is made like Jeroboam's, which he destroyed.
Gill: the denial of burial to Baasha's house is the very doom pronounced and executed upon Jeroboam's family.
Henry: the corpse-curse falls on the house, not the man, intimating a reckoning after death that the impenitent cannot escape.
Gill: the chronicler's formula points to fuller records, as with Jeroboam and Nadab.
The refrain of the 'book of the chronicles' is the compiler's editorial pointer beyond his own selective narrative.
Henry (over the passage): might and memorial are not acceptance; a name in the chronicles is not a name in the book of life.
Gill: Baasha died a natural, peaceful death and was honourably buried in Tirzah.
Henry: the chief sinner's peaceful death, escaping the denounced doom, proves a reckoning after death for the impenitent.
Gill (with Abarbanel): the repetition grounds the doom in Baasha's own evil works, not merely his making Israel to sin.
Ellicott: the historian's note, added for its last clause, shows Baasha's act, though foretold, was not thereby justified.
Clarke: 'because he killed him' is variously referred (Jehu, Jeroboam, or Nadab); the order is confused and v.7 may belong after v.4.

debates.question: Whom does 'because he killed him' denote?

sides.position: Nadab (or Jeroboam's house), whom Baasha destroyed
Jehu the prophet, per the Vulgate

themes: a foretold act not thereby justified
provoking with the work of his hands

schools.summary: Gill: Elah began to reign in Asa's twenty-sixth year and reigned two incomplete years, the very span Nadab son of Jeroboam had reigned.
The dating by Asa's regnal year and the inclusive two years fix the synchronism between the kingdoms and cohere with the following verses.
Zimri, captain of half the chariots, killed the drunken Elah in the steward Arza's house; the steward was likely complicit, and Zimri's name became a proverb for treachery.
Gill: Zimri commanded one of the two divisions of the royal war-chariots.
Henry: Elah, drunk while his army fought, is a warning that death comes easily and terribly upon men surprised in their sin.
Gill: Zimri smote the drunken Elah, killed him in Asa's twenty-seventh year, and reigned in his stead.
Keil and Delitzsch: Zimri at once destroyed all the males of Baasha's house, kin and friends, leaving no avenger of blood.

themes: destruction of the house of Baasha

schools.summary: Gill: Zimri began the slaughter at once, perhaps the very first day of his reign.
Clarke: he aimed to exterminate the whole race and blot out its memory, as the rabbis said extirpation reached even neighbouring houses.
The clause 'neither kinsfolk nor friends' means every male who might avenge the blood was cut off.
Henry: Zimri held by cruelty what he gained by treason, yet his name became a proverb of judgment.
Gill: the destruction reached all related and even befriended to Baasha, fulfilling the word of the Lord by Jehu.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'according to the word of the Lord' points back to v.1; the doom answers the named sins and idolatries of Baasha.

themes: the word of the Lord fulfilled

schools.summary: Gill: Elah shared his father's sins and so was cut off with him; the heir was a partner in the guilt.
Clarke: the verse shows why judgment fell — both kings were grievous offenders who made the people sin.
'Vanities' is the standing scriptural name for idols; idolatry is condemned as both an outrage against God and a pitiable folly wasted on nothings.

themes: the sins of Baasha and Elah

schools.summary: Gill: the chronicler's formula refers the rest of Elah's acts to the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel.
The recurring refrain marks the compiler's selective, theological purpose; Elah's notice, lacking even a mention of 'might,' is its own verdict.
Gill: Zimri reigned only seven days in Tirzah until Omri took the palace and destroyed him.
Clarke: the army besieging Gibbethon, hearing of the treason, made Omri king, who raised the siege to attack Tirzah.
Henry: a land's transgression multiplies its princes; forsaking God, Israel finds no rest as aspirants ruin one another and the Philistines gain.

themes: many princes for a land's sin
no rest for those who forsake God

schools.summary: The camp's acclamation of Omri shows the throne had become the prize of the sword; Baasha's dynasty too was founded before Gibbethon, and Zimri's ill-planned coup collapses at once.
Gill: the news of Zimri's treason and regicide reached and displeased the army.
Henry: recurring coups and civil wars are the sign of God's controversy with a sinning people, calling them to repent.

themes: the throne as prize of the sword

schools.summary: Gill: Omri raised the siege of Gibbethon and marched some thirty-six miles to besiege Zimri in Tirzah.
Zimri's coup lacked support, so Tirzah is invested at once with no battle in the field — the measure of seven days of stolen royalty.
Gill: unable to hold the ill-fortified Tirzah, Zimri burned the king's house over himself.
Ellicott: the word means the citadel; the desperate suicide, paralleled in Eastern history, marks how unpardonable his regicide was held to be.
Henry: Zimri's self-immolation shows how wickedness hurries men to their own ruin, an emblem of self-destroying sin.
Gill: his sins reach back over his whole life, not merely his last week; his death punishes the whole course.
Keil and Delitzsch: he 'died for his sins.' Ellicott: 'the way of Jeroboam' marks the kingdom's abiding idolatry, or a religious pretence Zimri dropped after the deed.

debates.question: How could a seven-day king 'walk in the way of Jeroboam'?

sides.position: the phrase marks the whole kingdom's abiding idolatry, which he did not repudiate
his coup wore a pretence of zeal for Jehu's prophecy, then cast off the religious mask

schools.summary: Gill: the record covers Zimri's acts before and during his reign, and the manner and success of his conspiracy.
The formula names 'his treason' where others speak of 'might' — the historian's phrasing is itself a verdict on a reign with no deeds but its crime.
Tibni, likely a military chief, contended with Omri; the throne was again decided by the army, and the struggle lasted about four years.
Gill: Israel divided into two parts over the succession.
Clarke: the reason for the split is unknown; the people favoured Tibni, the army Omri, and the army prevailed.
Henry: Tibni's party may have resisted a king chosen in the camp, wanting the succession settled by a convention rather than by the sword.
Keil and Delitzsch reckon Omri's twelve years from his rising against Zimri, with about four years of war and seven of sole rule; Ellicott notes the grim terseness of 'Tibni died, and Omri reigned.'
Gill: probably a battle was fought in which Tibni's followers were defeated.
Henry: the victor is no 'man of understanding' by whom a state is prolonged, but a soldier who outlasted his rivals to open a darker dynasty.
Gill: the twelve years are counted from Asa's twenty-seventh year, the four-year division preceding Omri's sole reign from the thirty-first.
Clarke: following Jarchi and the Seder Olam, the twenty-seventh and thirty-first years mark the start of the division and of the sole reign.
Ellicott: Omri opened a settled, prosperous epoch, allied with Tyre and ceding cities to Syria, yet threw away his chance to restore true worship.

debates.question: Why is Omri's accession dated to Asa's thirty-first year when Zimri fell in the twenty-seventh?

sides.position: the twelve years run from the twenty-seventh (proclamation); the thirty-first marks only the start of undisputed sole rule

schools.summary: Gill: Omri bought the hill for two talents of silver, about seven hundred and fifty pounds.
Named after Shemer, Samaria became the royal residence and burial-place until the Assyrian conquest; chosen by a soldier's eye, a 'watch-tower' of great natural strength that withstood long sieges.
Clarke: Omri moved his seat from fire-scarred Tirzah and founded Samaria, whose long after-history he traces to Herod's Sebaste and its present decay.
Gill: Omri wrought evil openly and publicly, as in defiance of God.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: he went beyond perpetuating the calves; the 'statutes of Omri' (Micah 6:16) became a byword, perhaps anticipating Ahab's Baal-worship.
Clarke: Omri's distinctive evil was to enact statutes compelling idolatry by law.
Henry: favoured in his rise, he proved worse than Jeroboam or Baasha, establishing iniquity by compulsion rather than mere allurement.
Gill: Omri walked in Jeroboam's way, worshipping the golden calves.
Keil and Delitzsch: the verse repeats verbatim the charge against Baasha and Elah (v.13), folding Omri into the same provocation with 'vanities.'
Gill: the chronicler records the rest of Omri's acts and 'his might,' as with the earlier kings.
Henry: many a bad man has been a stout man with a name in history, yet has no name in the book of life.
Gill: Omri died a natural death and was buried in Samaria, the city he had founded, Ahab succeeding him.
Henry (over the passage): the peaceful death of so great a sinner is the reprieve of a patient God, not the measure of his soul.
Keil and Delitzsch: Omri himself had prepared the way for Ahab's turn to worse evil.
Gill: Ahab began to reign in Asa's thirty-eighth year, the year of Omri's death, and reigned twenty-two years.
Henry: the worst of Israel's kings, with more recorded of him than any other; he hardened rather than took warning, making light of lesser sins and so opening the way to greater.

themes: lesser sins making way for greater

schools.summary: Gill: Ahab exceeded all by adding worse idols to the calves; the earlier kings feigned to worship the true God, but he served other gods.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'was it too little?' — unsatisfied with Jeroboam's calf-worship, Ahab married Jezebel and served Baal; Ethbaal was the priest-king of Tyre and Sidon.

themes: from corrupt worship to false gods

schools.summary: Gill: as if Jeroboam's calf-worship were too light a thing, Ahab went further by this marriage.
Clarke: Jezebel, the cruel idolatrous daughter of an idolatrous king who slew God's prophets, was the head and chief of Ahab's offending.

Ethbaal was a usurping priest-king of Tyre; the marriage was Ahab's fatal turning-point under Jezebel's relentless will, and the Baal-cult became a conflict of life and death with the worship of Jehovah.
Gill: Ahab built a temple and reared an altar to Baal in Samaria for convenience and his wife's sake — openly and daringly idolatrous.
Henry: he set Baal's house in the royal city to rival Jerusalem's temple, honouring and frequenting it — the folly of idolaters at pains to make a god.

themes: the temple and altar of Baal

schools.summary: Gill: the 'grove' is the Asherah/Astarte, an image Ahab made, its shade concealing the impurities of Baal-worship.
Clarke: the Asherah is Astarte or Venus, perhaps a Priapus set among tall trees.
Jamieson, Fausset and Brown: the act is a sad proof of the maniacal frenzy into which Ahab was transported.
Henry: the grove was made for concealment — he that doeth evil hateth the light — the furthest step of a deliberate descent that provoked the Lord.

themes: he that doeth evil hateth the light

schools.summary: Gill: Hiel rebuilt Jericho in ignorance or defiance of Joshua's curse, under Ahab's authority, hence its place among his evils.
Keil and Delitzsch: the sons' deaths fulfil Joshua's word (Joshua 6:26), proving God's word stands; Ellicott sets the rebuilding amid growing prosperity and Judah's half-dependence.
Clarke canvasses three readings of the curse — successive deaths, protracted labour, or child-sacrifice — inclining to delay and doubting the innocent sons suffered.
Henry: Hiel, like Achan meddling with the accursed thing, proves that those whom God curses are cursed indeed and none prospers who hardens his heart.

debates.question: How was Joshua's curse fulfilled in Hiel's sons?

sides.position: his children died successively, from the founding to the setting of the gates
the words denote only long, hindered labour over a lifetime
Hiel sacrificed his firstborn and youngest to consecrate the work
